food_subgroup: Japanese set meal with grilled fish, rice, miso soup, and side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for miso grilled halibut set meal; closest parent is grilled fish food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, grilled) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for miso-marinated grilled halibut set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for miso grilled halibut set meal.

preparation_method: served as set meal with steamed rice, miso soup, and side dishes
allergens: gluten (miso paste, sake, mirin may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Japanese set meal (teishoku) comprising approximately 150 g miso-grilled halibut fillet, 200 g steamed rice, 150 g miso soup, and 50 g assorted side dishes, totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central: Halibut, Atlantic and Pacific, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) combined with Japanese short-grain rice cooked and miso soup component estimates from USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete miso grilled halibut set meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted-component method: approximately 27% grilled halibut (USDA FDC 175136), 36% cooked Japanese short-grain rice (USDA SR Legacy), 27% miso soup (USDA FDC 172442 / FAO/INFOODS SEA), and 10% pickled/vegetable sides. Miso marinade contribution to sodium is included. Values represent a plausible central estimate for this set meal category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI reference value
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a complete miso grilled halibut set meal. The GI of the set meal is dominated by the steamed Japanese short-grain rice component (~36% by weight). Japanese short-grain cooked rice has a GI of approximately 54–68 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. A conservative central estimate of GI 54 is used for the composite set meal, reflecting the diluting effect of the high-protein halibut and miso soup components on the overall glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 90.75 g available carbohydrate per 550 g serving) / 100 = 48.8. Available carbohydrate per serving: 16.5 g/100 g × 550 g × 0.97 ≈ 88 g; GL = 54 × 88 / 100 ≈ 47.5, rounded to 48.8 using full carbohydrate basis.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from miso paste marinade and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this set meal as the miso marinade and miso soup contribute approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 2090 mg per full serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium miso or omitting the miso soup is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load from steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The set meal has a moderate-to-high glycaemic load (approximately 48 per serving) driven primarily by the steamed white rice; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller rice portion or substituting with brown rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursion, while the high-protein halibut component helps moderate glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from halibut offset by high sodium from miso
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Halibut provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein that support cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content from miso paste and miso soup is a concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and portion control or sodium reduction strategies are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Miso Grilled Halibut Set (味噌焼きヒラメ定食, Misoyaki Hirame Teishoku) is a traditional Japanese set meal (teishoku) featuring a halibut fillet that has been marinated in a mixture of white miso paste (shiro miso), mirin, and sake, then grilled until the surface is caramelised and lightly charred. Halibut (hirame in Japanese) is a lean, mild-flavoured flatfish prized in Japanese cuisine for its delicate texture and clean taste. The miso marinade imparts a savoury-sweet umami depth to the fish while keeping the flesh moist. The set meal is served with steamed Japanese short-grain rice, a bowl of miso soup, and assorted side dishes such as pickled vegetables (tsukemono) and simmered or blanched seasonal vegetables. This dish is a staple of Japanese teishoku-style dining and is commonly found in Japanese restaurants in Singapore, including at GyoGyo at Funan Mall. Nutritionally, the set provides a balanced macronutrient profile with lean protein from halibut (~8 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from rice (~16.5 g per 100 g), and low fat (~1.8 g per 100 g). Halibut is a good source of vitamin B12, niacin, selenium, and vitamin D. The primary nutritional concern is the elevated sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~2090 mg per full serving) contributed by the miso paste marinade and miso soup, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic load is moderate-to-high per serving due to the white rice component, making portion control of rice important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a miso grilled halibut set?
Is miso grilled fish high in sodium?
Is halibut set meal good for diabetes?
Japanese grilled fish set meal calories
Is miso grilled halibut high in protein?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete miso grilled halibut set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central data for grilled halibut and cooked Japanese rice, with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for miso soup. GI is estimated from the dominant rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated for the full set meal (halibut ~27%, rice ~36%, miso soup ~27%, sides ~10% by weight). 2. Sodium is elevated due to miso paste marinade and miso soup; actual sodium may vary by restaurant preparation. 3. GI of 54 is a conservative estimate based on the Japanese short-grain rice component (GI 54–68 per University of Sydney GI Database); the composite meal GI is lower than rice alone due to protein and fat dilution. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as halibut and miso-based ingredients contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for grilled halibut. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.5), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6) are estimated from USDA FDC halibut and rice component data. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 (grilled fish food product) is used as closest parent; no exact FoodOn term exists for miso grilled halibut set. 7. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, grilled) is used as closest parent. 8. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate for a standard Japanese restaurant set meal portion. 9. GL per serving = GI 54 × (16.5 g carbohydrate/100 g × 550 g serving / 100) = 54 × 90.75 / 100 ≈ 48.8. 10. The field dataed is GyoGyo - Funan, Singapore (field survey source). 11. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from component analysis; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
